result:
I’d give every child born today a secure trust of high-quality learning—think “personal Wikipedia + local mentor + broadband access” delivered on something as cheap and durable as a solar-powered phone.  
No strings attached, no warehouse of servers that can be nationalized, no central curriculum to dictate values—just a self-updating, offline-first vault of humanity’s best explanations, stories, simulations, and ways to practice. When they’re five it reads them bedtime stories in their parents’ dialect; at fifteen it walks them through open-source building plans for a foot-powered water pump; at twenty-five it connects them to others chasing the same problem on the other side of the planet.

The world already has all the raw knowledge; what’s missing is equitable access to it in a form that persists through wars, blackouts, censorship, poverty, and migration. Fixing that single distribution problem would compound every other change people might one day choose: better vaccines, cleaner energy, fairer economies, art no bureaucracy could have predicted.

prompt: If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
